title: From Plain CNNs to ResNets
concept: ResNets turn skip-connected residual blocks into a repeatable CNN design, letting depth grow much further than plain stacks usually allow without the same optimization pain.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why do skip connections help deep CNNs?

options.text: They preserve useful information and make gradient flow easier through depth
They replace all convolution with pooling
They work by removing the training objective

exercises.explain: Right — ResNets take that skip-connection idea and make it the default building pattern.

cards.title: A mountain trail becomes climbable
cards.body: A steep mountain is still a steep mountain, but switchbacks, bridges, and railings can turn it from dangerous to manageable. Very deep CNNs are similar: ResNets did not remove depth — they made that depth far more usable.

cards.title: What changes in a ResNet
cards.body: A plain CNN is mostly one long chain of layers. A ResNet rewires that chain into many residual blocks with skip connections. The convolutions are still there, but now each block can preserve its input and learn an added refinement. That change in wiring is what made very deep models like ResNet-50, ResNet-101, and beyond practical to train.

cards.title: Support beams on every floor
cards.body: If a builder adds more and more floors to a structure, support beams become essential. Without them, extra height can make the building fragile. ResNets add a repeatable support pattern to deep CNNs, so more layers are less likely to become architectural chaos.

cards.title: Same vision goal, smarter wiring
cards.body: Suppose you want a stronger image classifier and think more depth could help. A plain 50-layer chain may be difficult to optimize. A ResNet uses roughly the same deep-learning ingredients — convolutions, activations, feature maps — but organizes them as residual blocks so that depth becomes much more trainable.

cards.title: ResNet is not just 'a giant CNN'
cards.body: A common myth is that ResNets mattered only because they were bigger. But plain deep networks already showed that extra layers alone were not enough. The important shift was the residual design pattern: wiring the depth so it could actually be optimized well.

left.text:
Mostly one long stack.
Every block must carry the full signal chain.
Very deep versions can train poorly.

right.text:
Residual blocks repeat throughout.
Skip paths preserve and support signal flow.
Very deep models become far more usable.

exercises.prompt: What most clearly distinguishes a ResNet from a plain CNN of similar ingredients?

options.text: ResNet repeatedly uses residual blocks with skip connections instead of only a plain chain
ResNet can only classify black-and-white images
ResNet works only when convolution kernels are 1x1

exercises.explain: Correct — the breakthrough is the residual wiring pattern, not a brand-new kind of vision data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: ResNets mattered because they made deeper CNNs easier to train, not because depth alone suddenly became magical.
exercises.explain: True. ResNets changed whether deep CNNs were practically trainable, which is why they were such a big deal.

exercises.sentence: ResNets stack many residual ___ to make depth more usable.
exercises.explain: Blocks is right — the architecture repeats residual building blocks again and again.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best matches the move from a plain CNN to a ResNet?

options.text: A team keeps using convolutions, but rewires the model into many skip-connected residual blocks so deeper versions train more reliably
A team deletes all hidden layers and predicts directly from raw filenames
A team changes the image task into a spreadsheet task so depth no longer matters

exercises.explain: Exactly — ResNet is a structural redesign of the CNN stack, not a completely different machine-learning task.

exercises.prompt: A teammate says, 'So once we add skip connections, deep training is completely solved.' What is the best reply — and the best bridge to the next topic?

options.text: Skip connections made deep CNNs much easier to train, but deep models still benefit from other stabilizing tools too — next we'll study normalization layers, which help keep hidden activations well-behaved during training.
Yes. Skip connections removed the need for any other architectural or training choices forever.
No. ResNets only matter for image file storage, not for optimization.
Yes. Once a model has a skip path, activations and gradients can never become unstable again.

exercises.explain: Exactly. ResNets were a huge step because they made deep CNNs far more trainable, but they are not the only ingredient in stable training. The next structural helper to learn is normalization layers, which steady the hidden activations flowing through those deep networks.
